metadata.file_name: 82. Costa & Chen. (2023). Exploring the relationship between process data and contextual variables among Scandinavian students on PISA 2012 mathematics tasks.pdf
metadata.title: Exploring the relationship between process data and contextual variables among Scandinavian students on PISA 2012 mathematics tasks

survey_design.weight_fields_interpretation: The study uses PISA 2012 computer-based mathematics assessment (CBAM) log-file data for three publicly released items (CM015Q01, CM015Q02D, CM015Q03D) administered to 15-year-old students in Denmark, Sweden, and Norway. The analytic sample is restricted to students with available log-file data for the three items and then further reduced by excluding 188 students with missing values on the analysis variables, yielding 1426 students. For estimation, the authors incorporate PISA’s two-stage sampling unequal probabilities by applying sampling weights in CFA/latent regression using the R package lavaan.survey, which is designed for survey-weighted estimation. The manuscript does not report replicate-weight methods (e.g., BRR/jackknife), and the modeling is latent-variable CFA/latent regression rather than ML algorithms that would typically omit weights.

data.handling_not_reported_explanation: The dependent variables in this study are item-level response accuracy (binary/ordinal) and process indicators (response time and frequency of actions) modeled via CFA/latent regression; the paper does not use PISA achievement plausible values (PV) for the outcomes. Missingness is discussed for contextual questionnaire variables (2–34.7%) and the authors state they use full-information maximum likelihood (FIML) in parameter estimation rather than multiple imputation, but they do not provide a schema-level missing-data handling label for the analysis variables in a way that maps cleanly to the allowed categories. Therefore, missing-data handling is treated as not_reported in the extraction schema.

sample_details.sample_filtering_criteria: Used PISA 2012 computer-based mathematics assessment (CBAM) log-file data for three CD production items (CM015Q01, CM015Q02D, CM015Q03D) administered as a bundle with shared stimuli/interface. Restricted to Scandinavian countries: Denmark, Sweden, and Norway. Included only students with available log-file data for the three analyzed tasks, then removed 188 students with missing values, resulting in a final analytic sample of 1426 students.

confounders_identified.variable_name: ICT use at home for school tasks
Use of ICT in mathematics lessons

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 Computer-Based Mathematics (CBAM) — items CM015Q01, CM015Q02D, CM015Q03D (Scandinavia: DNK, SWE, NOR)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Latent regression coefficients (directional effects): ESCS positive across all three countries; gender significant for Denmark and Sweden but not Norway; ExPureM positive for Sweden and Norway but not Denmark; ICT negative for Denmark only. Model fit for multigroup latent regression: CFI=0.926, TLI=0.904, RMSEA=0.048, SRMR=0.058. CFA fit for three-factor model: CFI=0.982, TLI=0.957, RMSEA=0.052, SRMR=0.028.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 CBAM data for CM015Q01/CM015Q02D/CM015Q03D, the study leveraged ESCS, gender, language at home, immigration background, and contextual ICT/OTL variables to explain latent math performance (response accuracy), finding that ESCS consistently increased math proficiency across Denmark, Sweden, and Norway, while gender effects were country-specific (present in Denmark and Sweden only), ExPureM improved performance in Sweden and Norway only, and ICT showed a negative association with math performance in Denmark. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 Computer-Based Mathematics (CBAM) — items CM015Q01, CM015Q02D, CM015Q03D (Scandinavia: DNK, SWE, NOR)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Latent regression coefficients (directional effects): ESCS positive across all three countries; ExPureM positive for Norway only; ExApplM not consistently significant; ICT not significant for speed in any country. Model fit for multigroup latent regression: CFI=0.926, TLI=0.904, RMSEA=0.048, SRMR=0.058.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 CBAM data for CM015Q01/CM015Q02D/CM015Q03D, the study leveraged ESCS, gender, language at home, immigration background, and contextual ICT/OTL variables to explain latent speed (log response time), finding that higher ESCS was associated with longer response times in all three countries, while ExPureM increased speed only in Norway and ICT did not show a significant effect on speed. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 Computer-Based Mathematics (CBAM) — items CM015Q01, CM015Q02D, CM015Q03D (Scandinavia: DNK, SWE, NOR)
main_findings.target_variable: Latent exploration behaviour (log frequency of actions)
main_findings.performance_metrics: Latent regression coefficients (directional effects): ESCS positive for Denmark and Norway (not Sweden); gender significant for Sweden only (negative coefficient); ExPureM positive for Sweden and Norway; ExApplM positive for Denmark only; ICT negative for all three countries (largest magnitude in Denmark). Model fit for multigroup latent regression: CFI=0.926, TLI=0.904, RMSEA=0.048, SRMR=0.058.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 CBAM data for CM015Q01/CM015Q02D/CM015Q03D, the study leveraged ESCS, gender, language at home, immigration background, and contextual ICT/OTL variables to explain latent exploration behaviour (log frequency of actions), finding that ESCS increased exploration in Denmark and Norway, gender differences appeared only in Sweden, ExPureM increased exploration in Sweden and Norway, ExApplM increased exploration in Denmark, and ICT was negatively associated with exploration behaviour across the three countries. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

main_findings.dataset_used: PISA 2012 Computer-Based Mathematics (CBAM) — items CM015Q01, CM015Q02D, CM015Q03D (Scandinavia: DNK, SWE, NOR)
main_findings.target_variable: Measurement invariance of the three-factor process model
main_findings.performance_metrics: Measurement invariance comparison: weak invariance model preferred over configural/strong/strict based on fit and nested comparisons; weak invariance had CFI=0.968, RMSEA=0.075, SRMR=0.042, and was selected because it fit best among invariance levels. The weak invariance model allowed meaningful comparisons of factor covariance across Denmark, Sweden, and Norway.
main_findings.standardized_conclusion: Using PISA 2012 CBAM data for CM015Q01/CM015Q02D/CM015Q03D, the study evaluated measurement invariance of the latent process model across Denmark, Sweden, and Norway and found that weak measurement invariance (invariant factor loadings) performed best, enabling cross-country comparisons of the relationships among latent math performance, speed, and exploration behaviour. This indicates that these patterns have implications for educational policy and instructional practice, though cross-sectional ILSA designs cannot establish causality.

data.outcome_summary: This study analyzes PISA 2012 computer-based mathematics (CBAM) log-file process indicators and questionnaire context for three items (CM015Q01, CM015Q02D, CM015Q03D) from Denmark, Sweden, and Norway (final N=1426). A three-factor CFA model linking response accuracy, log response time, and log frequency of actions fit well (CFI=0.982, TLI=0.957, RMSEA=0.052, SRMR=0.028), and weak measurement invariance across countries was preferred, supporting meaningful cross-country comparisons. In the multigroup latent regression, ESCS showed a consistent positive association with math proficiency across all three countries, while ICT related negatively to math performance in Denmark and showed no effect on the latent factors in Sweden and Norway for some outcomes. Experience with pure mathematics tasks at school predicted higher achievement and more interaction with the platform in Sweden and Norway, and Norway showed no gender differences in proficiency, speed, or exploration. The paper is cross-sectional, so causal claims are not warranted; additionally, the extraction schema does not treat PVs as applicable because the outcomes are item-level accuracy and process indicators rather than PV-based achievement scores.
